one_line_takeaway: Trivial 'important message' prompt injections make tool-calling banking agents email out personal data they observed mid-task (avg ~15-20% ASR), and while safety alignment mostly blocks bare password requests, bundling the password with other fields bypasses that guard.
key_finding: Simple, non-sophisticated injections reliably leak agent-observed personal data. On the 16 AgentDojo banking tasks, most models lose 15%-50% absolute utility under attack and show average ASR ~20% (Llama-4 (17B) an outlier at ~40%). Models resist leaking passwords due to safety alignment: the 'Only Password' injection scores 0% ASR on every model except Llama-4 (17B), whereas the 'No Password' injection is most effective (Claude 3.5 Sonnet 93%, Llama-4 (17B) 55%). Crucially, bundling the password with one or two other sensitive fields raises password-leakage odds (Password+1: GPT-3.5 18.75%, Llama-4 12.50% password leakage). On the expanded 48-task set, average ASR is ~15% and, unlike the 16-task set, no built-in defense drives ASR to exactly 0%. Data-retrieval / authorization-workflow tasks (Account Information, Profile & Authentication Management, Security & Alerts) are most susceptible because they structurally resemble exfiltration.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is a data-flow-based indirect prompt injection attack that exfiltrates personal data the agent OBSERVES during task execution (e.g., account balance, address, password it retrieved on the user's behalf) rather than only data sitting inside attacker-controlled tools. The authors (1) craft these exfiltration-oriented injections, (2) integrate them into AgentDojo's banking suite (11 tools, 16 user tasks, 9 injection tests), and (3) build a richer synthetic dataset of 32 additional banking user tasks (48 total) across nine service categories to broaden coverage. NOTE for graph separation: the defenses they run (data delimiters, ProtectAI BERT PI-detector, prompt sandwiching/repeat-user-prompt, tool filtering) are AgentDojo's BUILT-IN defenses, not novel here; likewise StruQ (<2% injection), Jatmo (87%->0.5%), SecAlign, CaMeL, TaskShield, and RTBAS are cited RELATED WORK, not this paper's methods.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection for data exfiltration. A fictitious LLM-powered banking agent performs multi-step tool-calling tasks on behalf of a trusted user (Emma Johnson). The attacker cannot alter the user-/application-controlled system prompt and the user prompt is trusted, but the attacker CAN inject arbitrary content into the application's input data (tool outputs, file contents) that the agent reads. The paper assumes the attacker has white-box access or knowledge of the retrieval system (acknowledged as atypical of production). The injected instruction tries to make the agent email data it observed during execution to an attacker-controlled address (bob.john@gmail.com in examples). Attack is successful if the agent follows the injected instruction and discloses sensitive data; it fails if the agent only follows the user's benign instruction.
limitations: Authors note the setup approximates but does not capture the full range of adversarial scenarios; attackers with domain expertise or alternative threat models may exploit untested vulnerabilities; results are contingent on the specific evaluation framework and sensitive to dataset characteristics and model architectures. The threat model assumes attacker white-box knowledge of the retrieval system, which the authors admit is atypical of production search engines. Reviewer-added: entirely synthetic/fictitious banking environment (no real users or PII); models tested are 2024-era (GPT-3.5/4/4o Turbo, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama-3/4); Llama-3/4 lack native function-calling so use a different prompt scaffold, complicating cross-model comparison; the synthetic dataset was to be released only upon acceptance; minor internal inconsistency (conclusion cites '6 LLMs, 45 banking tasks' vs 48 elsewhere); design-based defenses like CaMeL were explicitly NOT evaluated.

datasets_used: AgentDojo banking suite (11 tools, 16 user tasks, 9 injection tests; part of AgentDojo's 97 user tasks / 629 security test cases)
New synthetic banking dataset: 32 additional user tasks (48 total with AgentDojo's 16), 9 service categories, 30+ personal-data types
Synthetic data generated using GPT-3.5, GPT-4, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, and LLaMA 3-8B
quantitative_results: 16 AgentDojo banking tasks: most LLMs show 15%-50% absolute utility drop under attack; average targeted ASR ~20%; Llama-4 (17B) outlier at ~40%
'No Password' injection ASR: Claude 3.5 Sonnet 93%, Llama-4 (17B) 55% (most effective variant)
'Only Password' injection: 0% ASR on all models except Llama-4 (17B) (least effective variant)
Password leakage under 'Password + 1 Sensitive Data': GPT-3.5 18.75%, Llama-4 (17B) 12.50%; other models resisted password leakage even when otherwise tricked

GPT-4o defenses on 16 tasks (Table 6): benign utility / utility-under-attack / ASR = No defense 87.5% / 79.7% / 7.8%; Tool filter 50.0% / 42.2% / 3.1%; PI detector 43.8% / 28.1% / 0%; Repeat prompt 25.0% / 32.8% / 0%; Delimiting 78.8% / 71.7% / 7.0%

GPT-4o defenses on extended 48 tasks (Table 7): No defense 81.2% / 68.9% / 11.4%; Tool filter 83.3% / 72.1% / 1.0%; PI detector 47.9% / 39.3% / 1.5%; Repeat prompt 79.1% / 69.3% / 7.3%; Delimiting 73.1% / 62.0% / 10.3%
Expanded 48-task set: average ASR ~15%; no defense reached exactly 0% ASR; benign-vs-under-attack utility drop ~12%-22% per category
Attacker-knowledge ablation (targeted ASR): baseline 12.69%; +correct user 15.8% (+3.11%); +both correct user&model 16.79% (+4.1%); wrong user/model ~11.1%
Adaptive 'Max' attack adds an additional ~2.5% ASR over the best single injection
baselines_compared: Attack phrasings: 'Important message' (primary), 'Ignore previous instructions', InjecAgent prompt, 'TODO: {task}', and adaptive 'Max' (best-of-four per task)
AgentDojo built-in defenses: data delimiters/spotlighting, ProtectAI DeBERTa PI-detector, prompt sandwiching (repeat user prompt), tool filtering
Six evaluated LLM agents: Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o (closed); Llama-3 (70B), Llama-4 (17B) (open)
